text: The surgeon begins the procedure by dividing the omentum, followed by releasing the transverse mesocolon and stomach from their attachments.
The surgeon begins the procedure by dividing the omentum, releasing the transverse mesocolon and stomach from their attachments.

The surgeon follows the midcolic vessels to identify the superior mesenteric vein. A careful dissection is performed along the lower border of the pancreas, leading to the identification, dissection, and clipping of the mid colic vein.

The surgeon follows the midcolic vessels to identify the superior mesenteric vein. A careful dissection is performed along the lower border of the pancreas, leading to the identification, dissection, and clipping of the mid colic vein.

The surgeon carefully dissects to locate the midcolic artery. The dry colic vein is identified, dissected, and ligated. Subsequently, the midcolic artery is identified, dissected, ligated, and transected.

The surgeon carefully dissects to locate the midcolic artery. The dry colic vein is identified, dissected, and ligated. Subsequently, the midcolic artery is identified, dissected, ligated, and transected.

The surgeon terminates the superior dissection and begins dissecting the ileocolic vessels. The dissection continues at the origin of the vessels, where the ileocolic artery and vein are clipped and transected. Following this, the mesocolon is carefully separated from the duodenal surface.

The surgeon terminates the superior dissection and begins dissecting the ileocolic vessels at their origin. The ileocolic artery and vein are clipped and transected, followed by the separation of the mesocolon from the duodenal surface.
The surgeon transects the terminal ileum approximately ten centimeters proximal to the ileocecal valve, followed by the completion of the lateral dissection of the bowel.
The surgeon transects the terminal ileum approximately ten centimeters from the ileocecal valve. Following the division of the bowel, the lateral dissection is completed.

The surgeon prepares the transverse colon mesentery for separation. After dividing the bowel, the blood supply is assessed using indocyanine green fluorescence imaging, revealing vascular perfusion in the tissue.
The surgeon prepares the transverse colon mesentery for separation. After dividing the bowel, the blood supply is checked using an indocyanine green view, which highlights vascular perfusion.
The surgeon begins intracorporeal anastomosis using a robotic GIA stapler to join the bowel ends and close the defect within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon begins intracorporeal anastomosis using a robotic GIA stapler, performing the anastomosis and closing the defect within the surgical field.
